title: Build a Local Ollama ChatGPT CLI in Python
tagline: A beginner‑friendly project that lets you chat with a locally‑run Ollama model from the terminal.
description: In this tutorial you will create a tiny command‑line chat application that talks to an Ollama server running on your machine. You’ll learn how to structure a modern Python project (pyproject.toml, src layout, pathlib, type hints), make HTTP calls with httpx, handle JSON responses, and write a simple pytest suite. No classes, no external charting libraries – just plain functions and clear, incremental steps.
problem_statement: You have installed Ollama locally and want a quick way to interact with a language model without opening a web UI. The goal is to build a reusable, well‑structured CLI that sends a prompt to the Ollama API and prints the model’s reply.
end_state: A runnable `python -m src.main` command that opens a REPL‑style chat loop, a small test suite, and a clean, PEP‑8 compliant repository ready for further extension.
prerequisites: Ollama installed and a model pulled (e.g., `ollama pull llama3.2`)
Basic familiarity with the terminal and pip
learning_outcomes: Create a modern Python project with pyproject.toml and src layout
Use pathlib for file system paths
Make async HTTP requests with httpx
Write type‑annotated functions and handle JSON payloads

stack.name: httpx
stack.purpose: Async HTTP client for Ollama API

best_practices: Keep all runtime code inside the `src/` package; never import from the repository root.
Use `pathlib.Path` instead of string paths for OS‑independent file handling.
Add type hints to every public function; run `mypy` locally if you like.
Separate configuration (model name, host) into its own module for easy tweaking.
Write small, pure functions that can be unit‑tested without network calls.
intro_transcript: Hey everyone, welcome back to the channel! Today we’re going to build something that feels a lot like ChatGPT, but it runs entirely on your own machine using Ollama. No cloud keys, no hidden endpoints – just a tiny Python script that talks to the Ollama server you already have installed. This project is perfect for beginners because we’ll start from an empty folder, set up a modern Python project structure, write a few straightforward functions, and end up with a REPL‑style chat loop you can run from the terminal. Along the way we’ll cover best practices like using `pyproject.toml`, the `src/` layout, type hints, and a minimal pytest suite. By the end of the video you’ll have a working CLI that you can extend however you like – maybe add logging, a GUI, or even integrate with other tools. So grab your favorite code editor, make sure Ollama is up and running, and let’s dive in!

steps.title: Initialize the repository and pyproject
steps.goal: Create a clean project skeleton with modern packaging metadata.
bullets: Create a new folder called `local-ollama-chat`
Run `git init` and add a .gitignore for __pycache__ and .venv
Create `pyproject.toml` with build-system and dependencies
Add an empty `src/__init__.py` to make it a package
steps.speaker_notes: Alright, first things first – let’s get our repository set up. Open a terminal and make a new directory called `local-ollama-chat`. Inside that folder we’ll run `git init` so we can track our changes. I always add a `.gitignore` right away; it should ignore the typical Python artefacts like `__pycache__/`, `.venv/`, and `*.pyc`. Next we create a `pyproject.toml`. This file replaces the old `setup.py` and tells tools like pip and poetry how to build the project. We’ll specify the minimum Python version, list `httpx` as a runtime dependency, and add `pytest` under the `[tool.poetry.dev-dependencies]` section – even though we’re not using Poetry, the format works for pip‑install‑editable. Finally, we add an empty `src/__init__.py`. This file tells Python that `src` is a package, which is required for the import system when we run `python -m src.main`. Once you’ve saved the file, run `pip install -e .` to install the project in editable mode. This step ensures that any changes you make to the source files are instantly reflected when you run the code. Take a moment to verify the installation with `python -c "import src; print(src.__name__)"`. If you see `src`, you’re good to go. That’s it for the first step – we now have a clean, version‑controlled project ready for code.

code.code:
[build-system]
requires = ["setuptools>=70.0", "wheel"]
build-backend = "setuptools.build_meta"

[project]
name = "local-ollama-chat"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "A tiny CLI that talks to a local Ollama model"
requires-python = ">=3.12"
dependencies = [
    "httpx>=0.27.0"
]

[project.optional-dependencies]
dev = [
    "pytest>=8.2.2"
]
code.explanation: Defines the build system, project metadata, runtime dependency on httpx, and dev dependency on pytest.
code.best_practice_notes: Using `pyproject.toml` keeps the project future‑proof and works with both pip and modern build tools.

steps.title: Add configuration constants
steps.goal: Centralise Ollama host and model name so they can be changed without touching code.
bullets: Create `src/config.py`
Define `OLLAMA_HOST` as a pathlib Path pointing to http://localhost:11434
Define `DEFAULT_MODEL` (e.g., "llama3.2")
Export `__all__` for tidy imports
steps.speaker_notes: Now that the project skeleton is ready, let’s give ourselves a place to store configuration values. Hard‑coding URLs and model names inside functions makes future changes painful, so we’ll create a tiny `config.py` module. Inside we’ll use `pathlib.Path` to build the base URL – even though it’s a string, wrapping it in `Path` shows the modern approach and lets us easily join additional path segments later. We’ll also define a `DEFAULT_MODEL` constant that matches the model you pulled with Ollama, for example `llama3.2`. Exporting `__all__` is optional but it signals to readers which names are part of the public API of this module. Once you save the file, you can import these constants from anywhere in the project, keeping the code DRY and making it trivial to switch to a different model or host later on.

code.code:
from pathlib import Path

# Base URL for the local Ollama server. Change the port if you configured a custom one.
OLLAMA_HOST: str = "http://localhost:11434"

# The model you want to chat with. Make sure you have pulled it with `ollama pull`.
DEFAULT_MODEL: str = "llama3.2"

__all__ = ["OLLAMA_HOST", "DEFAULT_MODEL"]
code.explanation: Provides two constants: the host URL and the default model name, both type‑annotated as strings.
code.best_practice_notes: Using pathlib for URLs is optional but demonstrates a consistent path handling style across the project.

steps.title: Write the low‑level Ollama client
steps.goal: Create a function that sends a prompt to Ollama and returns the generated text.
bullets: Create `src/ollama_client.py`
Define `async def generate(prompt: str, model: str = DEFAULT_MODEL) -> str`
Use httpx.AsyncClient to POST to `/api/generate`
Parse the JSON response and return the `response` field
steps.speaker_notes: The heart of our application is the function that talks to Ollama. Ollama exposes a simple HTTP API – you POST a JSON payload to `/api/generate` with a `model` and a `prompt`, and it streams back a JSON object containing a `response` string. We’ll write an async function called `generate` that takes a prompt and an optional model name, builds the request payload, and returns the generated text. Using `httpx.AsyncClient` gives us non‑blocking I/O, which is a good habit even for a tiny script. Inside the function we’ll construct the URL by joining `OLLAMA_HOST` with the endpoint, then call `client.post` with a JSON body. Ollama streams the response, but for simplicity we’ll read the whole response at once with `await resp.json()`. Finally we extract the `response` key and return it. If anything goes wrong – network error, non‑200 status, or missing key – we raise a `RuntimeError` with a helpful message. This function is pure enough to be unit‑tested by mocking the HTTP call, which we’ll cover later. Let’s write the code now.

code.code:
import json
from typing import Any
import httpx
from .config import OLLAMA_HOST, DEFAULT_MODEL

async def generate(prompt: str, model: str = DEFAULT_MODEL) -> str:
    """Send *prompt* to the Ollama server and return the generated text.

    Args:
        prompt: The user’s message.
        model: The model name; defaults to the value from ``config.DEFAULT_MODEL``.

    Returns:
        The model's response as a plain string.
    """
    url = f"{OLLAMA_HOST}/api/generate"
    payload: dict[str, Any] = {"model": model, "prompt": prompt}
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=30.0) as client:
        response = await client.post(url, json=payload)
        if response.status_code != 200:
            raise RuntimeError(
                f"Ollama API returned {response.status_code}: {response.text}"
            )
        data = response.json()
        if "response" not in data:
            raise RuntimeError("Malformed Ollama response: missing 'response' key")
        return data["response"]

__all__ = ["generate"]
code.explanation: Defines an async `generate` function that posts a prompt to Ollama and returns the `response` field, with error handling for non‑200 status and missing keys.
code.best_practice_notes: Async I/O keeps the CLI responsive; explicit error messages make debugging easier.

steps.title: Create the interactive CLI loop
steps.goal: Build a REPL that reads user input, calls the client, and prints the answer.
bullets: Create `src/main.py`
Define `def chat_loop() -> None` that runs forever until EOF or "exit"
Use `asyncio.run` to call the async `generate` function
Print the model response with a clear prefix
steps.speaker_notes: With the client in place we can now build the user‑facing part of the app – a simple read‑eval‑print loop. In `main.py` we’ll define a `chat_loop` function that repeatedly prompts the user for input using Python’s built‑in `input`. If the user types `exit` or sends an EOF (Ctrl‑D), we break out of the loop and say goodbye. For each line we call `asyncio.run(generate(user_input))` – this is the easiest way to bridge synchronous code (the REPL) with our async client without pulling in a full event‑loop manager. The response is printed with a `ChatGPT:` prefix so it’s clear who is speaking. At the bottom of the file we add the usual `if __name__ == "__main__":` guard that invokes `chat_loop()`. This keeps the module importable for testing while still being executable as a script. Let’s write the code and run a quick test – type a short question like “What is the capital of France?” and watch the model answer. If you see a response, congratulations – you’ve just built a functional local ChatGPT!

code.explanation: Implements a REPL that reads user input, calls the async `generate` function via `asyncio.run`, and prints the model's reply. Handles EOF and simple error reporting.
code.expected_output: When run, the program prints a welcome banner, then for each line typed shows `ChatGPT: <model response>`.
code.best_practice_notes: Separating I/O helpers (`_print_welcome`, `_prompt_user`) makes the loop easier to test and keeps the main logic clean.

steps.title: Add basic unit test for the client
steps.goal: Demonstrate how to test the `generate` function with a mocked HTTP response.
bullets: Create `tests/test_ollama_client.py`
Use `pytest` and `httpx.MockTransport` to fake Ollama’s JSON reply
Patch `httpx.AsyncClient` with the mock transport
Assert that `generate` returns the expected string
steps.speaker_notes: Even though our project is tiny, writing a test shows good habits early. We’ll place the test under the `tests/` folder so pytest discovers it automatically. The trick is that `generate` uses `httpx.AsyncClient`, which performs real network I/O. To avoid hitting a live Ollama server during testing, we’ll use `httpx.MockTransport`. This class lets us define a callable that receives the request and returns a fabricated `httpx.Response`. In the test we’ll create a transport that returns a JSON payload with a `response` field containing the string "mocked answer". Then we’ll monkey‑patch `httpx.AsyncClient` to use this transport via the `monkeypatch` fixture. Finally we call `await generate("test")` inside an `asyncio` event loop (pytest supports async tests natively) and assert that the result matches our mock. Running `pytest -q` should show one passed test. This gives you confidence that the client correctly parses the JSON structure and raises errors when needed.

code.code:
import json
import pytest
import httpx
from src.ollama_client import generate

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_generate_returns_response(monkeypatch):
    # Prepare a mock transport that returns a fixed JSON payload.
    async def mock_send(request: httpx.Request) -> httpx.Response:
        payload = {"response": "mocked answer"}
        return httpx.Response(200, json=payload)

    transport = httpx.MockTransport(mock_send)

    # Patch the AsyncClient to use our mock transport.
    class MockAsyncClient(httpx.AsyncClient):
        def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
            super().__init__(transport=transport, *args, **kwargs)

    monkeypatch.setattr("httpx.AsyncClient", MockAsyncClient)

    result = await generate("any prompt")
    assert result == "mocked answer"
code.explanation: Uses httpx.MockTransport to simulate Ollama's API, patches httpx.AsyncClient, and verifies that `generate` returns the mocked response.
code.best_practice_notes: Mocking external services keeps tests fast and deterministic.

steps.title: Run the full application
steps.goal: Verify that the CLI talks to a real Ollama server and passes the test suite.
bullets: Start Ollama (`ollama serve` if not already running)
Run `pytest` to ensure the mock test passes
Execute `python -m src.main` and have a short conversation
Observe graceful handling of network errors
steps.speaker_notes: Now it’s time to see everything work together. First, make sure the Ollama daemon is up – you can start it with `ollama serve` or simply run `ollama list` to confirm the server is listening on port 11434. Next, run the test suite with `pytest -q`. You should see a single passed test, confirming that our client correctly parses the mocked JSON. Finally, launch the chat application by running `python -m src.main`. You’ll see the welcome banner, then you can type a question like “Tell me a joke.” The script will call the real Ollama model you pulled earlier and print the answer. Try typing an empty line – the loop will just prompt again. To test error handling, stop the Ollama server and send a prompt; you should see a friendly error message instead of a traceback. This demonstrates that we’ve built a robust, user‑friendly CLI in under an hour. Feel free to experiment with different prompts or swap the model name in `config.py` to see how the responses change.

steps.title: Add a tiny helper for multi‑line prompts
steps.goal: Allow the user to enter a block of text terminated by a line containing only `---`.
bullets: Update `_prompt_user` in `src/main.py`
Collect lines until a separator line is entered
Join the lines with newline characters
Document the new feature in the welcome banner
steps.speaker_notes: Sometimes you want to feed the model a longer piece of text – maybe a paragraph you copied from a document. To make that easy, we’ll extend the REPL so that if you type a line containing only three hyphens (`---`), the loop will treat everything typed before that line as a single multi‑line prompt. We’ll modify the `_prompt_user` helper to keep reading `input()` until it sees the separator, then return the concatenated string. The welcome message will be updated to mention this new capability. This change only touches `src/main.py` and does not affect the client or tests, so you can keep the same test suite. After implementing, run the script again, type a few lines, end with `---`, and watch the model respond to the whole block. This demonstrates how you can evolve a simple CLI without breaking existing functionality.

code.explanation: Replaces the simple `input` call with a loop that collects lines until a line containing only `---` is entered, enabling multi‑line prompts.
code.best_practice_notes: Keeping I/O logic separate makes future extensions (e.g., reading from a file) straightforward.

steps.title: Wrap‑up and next steps
steps.goal: Summarise what was built and suggest extensions.
bullets: Recap project structure and key concepts
Mention possible improvements: logging, streaming responses, config file
Encourage viewers to publish the repo and experiment with other models
Invite feedback and subscription
steps.speaker_notes: Congratulations! You just built a fully functional local ChatGPT client in under two hours. We started with a clean repository, added a modern `pyproject.toml`, wrote a tiny configuration module, implemented an async Ollama client, built a REPL loop, added a unit test, and even extended the input handling for multi‑line prompts. The final layout lives under `src/` and is ready for further growth – you could replace the simple `print` statements with a proper logger, stream the model’s output token‑by‑token for a more interactive feel, or read the model name from a YAML config file. If you’re feeling adventurous, try swapping `llama3.2` for a different model, or integrate a small web UI with FastAPI. Don’t forget to push your code to GitHub, add a README, and share it with the community. As always, if you liked this tutorial, hit the like button, subscribe for more hands‑on projects, and let me know in the comments what you’d like to see next. Happy coding!

outro_transcript: Thanks for watching! In this video we turned a few lines of Python into a usable local ChatGPT that runs entirely on your machine. You now have a solid foundation – a clean project layout, type‑annotated functions, async HTTP calls, and a test suite you can expand. The next logical steps are to add proper logging, handle streaming responses for a more natural feel, or even wrap the CLI in a tiny web service using FastAPI. If you run into any issues, drop a comment below or open an issue on the GitHub repo linked in the description. Don’t forget to star the repo if you found it helpful, and subscribe for more beginner‑friendly projects. Until next time, keep building!
how_to_run:
1. Ensure Ollama is running locally (`ollama serve`).
2. Pull a model, e.g., `ollama pull llama3.2`.
3. Clone the repository and `cd` into it.
4. Create a virtual environment: `python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate`.
5. Install the project in editable mode: `pip install -e .`.
6. Run the test suite to verify everything works: `pytest -q`.
7. Start the chat application: `python -m src.main`.
8. Type your prompt, end multi‑line input with `---`, or type `exit` to quit.

challenges.title: Streaming token‑by‑token output
challenges.description: Modify the client to use Ollama's streaming endpoint (`/api/generate`) and print each token as it arrives, creating a more interactive feel.
challenges.build_process:
Replace the existing `src/ollama_client.py` with the code above, then update `src/main.py` to use `generate_stream` inside the REPL:
```python
async for token in generate_stream(user_input):
    print(token, end="", flush=True)
print()  # newline after the full response
```
challenges.solution_code:
{'language': 'python', 'file_path': 'src/ollama_client.py', 'code': 'import json
from typing import Any, AsyncGenerator
import httpx
from .config import OLLAMA_HOST, DEFAULT_MODEL

async def generate_stream(prompt: str, model: str = DEFAULT_MODEL) -> AsyncGenerator[str, None]:
    """Yield tokens from Ollama as they are streamed.
    
    This uses the same `/api/generate` endpoint but with `stream: true` in the payload.
    Each chunk of the response contains a JSON object with a `response` field that
    holds the next token (or partial text). The generator yields the raw text pieces.
    """
    url = f"{OLLAMA_HOST}/api/generate"
    payload: dict[str, Any] = {"model": model, "prompt": prompt, "stream": True}
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=30.0) as client:
        async with client.stream("POST", url, json=payload) as resp:
            async for line in resp.aiter_lines():
                if not line:
                    continue
                try:
                    data = json.loads(line)
                    if "response" in data:
                        yield data["response"]
                except json.JSONDecodeError:
                    continue

# Updated `generate` to call the streaming version for backward compatibility
async def generate(prompt: str, model: str = DEFAULT_MODEL) -> str:
    chunks = []
    async for token in generate_stream(prompt, model):
        chunks.append(token)
    return "".join(chunks)

__all__ = ["generate", "generate_stream"]
', 'explanation': "Adds `generate_stream` that yields partial responses using httpx's streaming API, and rewrites `generate` to collect the stream into a full string.", 'expected_output': None, 'best_practice_notes': 'Streaming reduces latency and mimics the feel of real‑time chat.'}

challenges.title: External JSON configuration file
challenges.description: Move `OLLAMA_HOST` and `DEFAULT_MODEL` into a `config.json` file and load them at runtime.
challenges.build_process:
Create a `src/config.json` file with content:
```json
{
  "ollama_host": "http://localhost:11434",
  "default_model": "llama3.2"
}
```\nRun the application again; it will read the values from this file.
